summary: In this brief post, Joel Spolsky shares a quote from his interview with ACM Queue comparing small and large companies. He uses a hotel analogy: big companies are like the Bellagio — nice but impersonal — while small companies are like bed and breakfasts, offering a more personal experience with both upsides and downsides. The analogy captures how small companies can be wonderfully intimate or disturbingly eccentric, with no middle ground.
key_points: Large companies offer polish and reliability but lack personal connection — nobody remembers your name
Small companies provide a more intimate, personalized experience where relationships matter
The downside of small companies is unpredictability — the quirks can be charming or deeply uncomfortable
Company culture at small scale is a high-variance bet compared to the standardized big-company experience
money_quotes: Basically a small company has a flavor to it, whereas a big company is sort of like checking into the Bellagio in Las Vegas.
It's a nice hotel but it has 5,000 rooms, so don't expect anybody to remember your name.
A small company is more like a bed and breakfast. You're going to have a great time because you get along with people and it's a much friendlier experience.
You don't really mind that the bathroom is down the hall because the people made a special vegetarian meal for you and then showed you around town.
On the other hand, you might be at a bed and breakfast where they have weird leather implements and lots of cats.
key_insight: Small companies are high-variance experiences — they have a distinctive flavor that can be either wonderfully personal or uncomfortably eccentric, unlike the reliable but anonymous uniformity of large companies.
